title: Choosing an Engine: PCA, EFA, and ESEM
text: PCA extracts principal components — linear combinations observed variables capture maximum variance, including measurement error. Every item modeled communality 1.0: components account 100% item’s variance. true latent variable model; data reduction method. bass-ackwards context, PCA natural default. fast, always converges, produces eigenvalues can guide choice k. Waller (2007) showed -level algebra (W'RW) holds exactly components, making edges algebraically exact rather approximated materialized scores. “fit” PCA just eigenvalue component — amount variance captures. chi-square tests, RMSEA, model rejection.
code: x_pca <- ackwards(bfi, k_max = 3, cor = "polychoric") x_pca #>  #> ── Bass-Ackwards Analysis (ackwards) ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: pca #> Rotation: varimax #> Basis: polychoric #> n: 875 #> k (max): 3 #>  #> ── Levels ── #>  #> ✔ k = 1: 1 factor, 23.2% variance #> ✔ k = 2: 2 factors, 35.5% variance #> ✔ k = 3: 3 factors, 44.6% variance #>  #> ── Edges ── #>  #> 5 of 8 edges have |r| ≥ 0.3 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: This is a series of linked solutions, not a fitted hierarchical model. #> Cross-level edges are descriptive score correlations. Per-level fit indices #> (EFA/ESEM) describe how well a k-factor model fits the items at that level -- #> they do not validate the edges or the hierarchy itself. tidy(x_pca, what = "fit") #>   level       statistic    value #> 1     1 eigenvalue.m1f1 5.802803 #> 2     2 eigenvalue.m2f1 5.802803 #> 3     2 eigenvalue.m2f2 3.067627 #> 4     3 eigenvalue.m3f1 5.802803 #> 5     3 eigenvalue.m3f2 3.067627 #> 6     3 eigenvalue.m3f3 2.275419

what: ESEM: EFA with full model diagnostics
title: Choosing an Engine: PCA, EFA, and ESEM
text: ESEM (exploratory structural equation modeling, Asparouhov & Muthén, 2009) fits common-factor model EFA uses lavaan engine. ESEM ordinal-tool: handles continuous items maximum-likelihood estimators (ML, MLR — default continuous data) ordinal items WLSMV, unlocks three capabilities EFA provide: Standard errors every loading, enabling confidence intervals significance tests — continuous ordinal data. Full maximum-likelihood estimation continuous data (ML/MLR), including FIML missing data (missing = "fiml"), uses partially observed rows rather deleting . WLSMV estimator ordinal data, appropriate maximum-likelihood-adjacent estimator categorical indicators. cor = "polychoric" set engine = "esem", WLSMV used automatically. three reasons pay ESEM’s cost (lavaan fit per level, occasional convergence trouble). EFA otherwise first-class reporting engine: returns loadings, variance, -level edges plus per-level RMSEA TLI — enough report hierarchy. Reach ESEM specifically need loading standard errors (especially smaller n), field-standard WLSMV estimator ordinal indicators, true FIML missing data; otherwise EFA simpler, faster choice. ESEM fit indices include CFI SRMR addition RMSEA TLI, giving richer picture model adequacy. See “Per-level fit” section report interpret indices.
code: x_esem <- ackwards(bfi, k_max = 3, engine = "esem", cor = "polychoric") x_esem #>  #> ── Bass-Ackwards Analysis (ackwards) ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: esem #> Rotation: varimax #> Basis: polychoric #> n: 875 #> k (max): 3 #>  #> ── Levels ── #>  #> ✔ k = 1: 1 factor, 23.5% variance #> ✔ k = 2: 2 factors, 32.9% variance #> ✔ k = 3: 3 factors, 39.5% variance #>  #> ── Edges ── #>  #> 5 of 8 edges have |r| ≥ 0.3 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: This is a series of linked solutions, not a fitted hierarchical model. #> Cross-level edges are descriptive score correlations. Per-level fit indices #> (EFA/ESEM) describe how well a k-factor model fits the items at that level -- #> they do not validate the edges or the hierarchy itself.

text: Bass-ackwards analyses often involve large item pools, ESEM expensive engine fits separate lavaan model every level. what: The problem: which factor solution is right?
title: Introduction to Bass-Ackwards Analysis
text: Anyone applied factor analysis faces dilemma: many factors extract? Parallel analysis might say 5; scree plot might suggest 3; reviewer might insist 1. solution seems describe data differently, tempting treat competing answers question. Goldberg (2006) reframed problem: different solutions competing — complementary. 1-factor solution captures broadest shared variance data, dimension along variables correlate. 5-factor solution captures narrower, specific dimensions. two levels resolution describe underlying structure different vantage points, just satellite image street map describe city. bass-ackwards method makes relationship explicit. fits factor models every level 1 k, computes correlations factor scores across adjacent levels. correlations show : narrow factor inherits broad factor — lineage dimension move coarse fine resolution. factor splits — single broad factor correlates strongly two narrower factors fragmenting sub-dimensions. factor stable — factor correlates ~1.0 counterpart adjacent level essentially construct appearing two resolutions. result hierarchy map — visual numerical account factor structure data builds broad narrow. Note: descriptive, data-driven hierarchy, confirmatory hierarchical model like Schmid–Leiman higher-order SEM. -level correlations score correlations (algebraic equivalents), model parameters.

See ?bfi25 full provenance; data derive SAPA project using public-domain IPIP items. drop cases missing item. real analysis might use pairwise deletion multiple imputation; na.omit() sufficient illustration.

title: Introduction to Bass-Ackwards Analysis
text: Now fit bass-ackwards hierarchy. important arguments : BFI items ordinal, use cor = "polychoric". computes polychoric correlations items factor extraction, giving accurate representation latent structure. ackwards() repeat ordinal-detection warning suggest_k() raised: setting cor = "polychoric" exactly heed , telling ackwards() accounted ordinal measurement scale. varimax? Within level, ackwards() rotates factors orthogonally using varimax, merely cosmetic default. Orthogonality gives -level score correlations closed form: orthonormal rotation satisfies T′ = T⁻¹, exactly makes W′RW edge algebra exact rather approximation. Varimax orthogonal rotation Goldberg (2006) used, rotation “CF-VARIMAX” reported Mplus-based papers Kim & Eaton (2015) — CF(κ = 1/p) varimax — two labels competing choices. Oblique rotations deliberately offered: correlated factors confound cross-level signal method exists measure.
code: x <- ackwards(bfi, k_max = 5, cor = "polychoric")

title: Introduction to Bass-Ackwards Analysis
text: hierarchy diagram centerpiece method. row represents one level (k = 1 top, k = 5 bottom). Arrows connect factor primary parent — factor level strongest correlation (|r|). Arrow thickness proportional |r|, color shows direction (blue = positive, red = negative default); aesthetics come legend. clean solution every drawn edge positive, blue appears . left--right layout (handy wide slides), pass direction = "horizontal". Reading diagram broad (top) narrow (bottom) tells story Big Five: k = 1: One broad factor. Given polychoric basis, captures shared variance across 25 items. k = 2: broad factor splits two: Neuroticism separates mixed cluster Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Openness items (Extraversion items load strongly within cluster). k = 3: non-Neuroticism cluster splits , separating Extraversion/Agreeableness Conscientiousness/Openness; Neuroticism stays intact sheds residual cross-loadings. k = 4: Conscientiousness Openness differentiate factors. k = 5: Extraversion Agreeableness finally differentiate, completing canonical Big Five. factors labeled m{k}f{j} (level k, factor j). stable IDs used throughout object, m5f1 k = 5 refers factor loadings, edge table, factor scores, diagram.

title: Introduction to Bass-Ackwards Analysis

Fitting half defensible analysis. recommended workflow — gates hierarchy depth split-half replicability comparability() flags non-differentiating factors prune() interpret — see vignette("ackwards-girard").

what: Why Pearson underestimates for ordinal items
title: Ordinal Data: Polychoric Correlations and WLSMV
text: continuous latent trait sliced small number ordered categories, observed correlation two items always lower correlation underlying latent variables. coarse rating scale, larger attenuation. 5-point scale, Pearson correlations can 10–20% lower true latent correlations; 2-point (binary) scale, underestimate can exceed 30%. matters bass-ackwards analysis : Loadings attenuated. Factors appear weaker . -level edges attenuated. hierarchy looks flatter — factors nearly perfectly correlated across levels may appear moderately correlated. number factors underestimated. Attenuation compresses eigenvalue spectrum, selection criteria (parallel analysis, MAP) may suggest fewer factors truly exist.

title: Ordinal Data: Polychoric Correlations and WLSMV
text: polychoric correlation two ordinal items estimates Pearson correlation exist underlying continuous latent variables, variables measured without discretization. fitting bivariate normal model thresholds category boundary. binary (two-category) items estimate traditionally called tetrachoric correlation, separate method — tetrachoric simply two-category special case polychoric. cor = "polychoric" handles binary items automatically: psych::polychoric() detects two-level case returns tetrachoric estimate, never request tetrachoric separately. Setting cor = "polychoric" ackwards() replaces step 1 pipeline — computing item correlation matrix R — polychoric matrix. downstream computation (factor extraction, rotation, tenBerge scoring, -level algebra) operates polychoric R.

Two edge aesthetics carry -level correlations, one comes legend: arrow thickness encodes magnitude |r|, edge color encodes direction (blue = positive, red–orange = negative). title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis R ackwards implements Goldberg’s (2006) bass-ackwards method modern extensions mapping hierarchical structure multivariate data. core insight simple: instead asking many factors best describe data, ask solutions different levels resolution related one another. 1-factor solution captures broadest shared variance; 5-factor solution captures narrower, specific dimensions. Bass-ackwards analysis traces broad factors split narrow ones — narrow factors redundant re-combinations broader ones. package supports three extraction engines (PCA, EFA, ESEM) polychoric correlations ordinal data. Beyond fitting, full analysis toolkit: suggest_k() brackets plausible depth, comparability() gates depth split-half replicability, Forbes’s (2023) prune() flags redundant artifactual factors skip-level connections, boot_edges() puts bootstrap confidence intervals every edge, predict() scores new observations sample.

what: Step 1 — Suggest a range of k
title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: suggest_k() runs five complementary criteria — two forms parallel analysis (PC FA basis), MAP, VSS, optionally Comparison Data — help choose upper bound hierarchy depth. criteria converge consensus range covers k = 5, consistent known Big Five structure instrument.
code: library(ackwards)  sk <- suggest_k(bfi25) sk #>  #> ── Factor / Component Count Suggestion (ackwards) ────────────────────────────── #> Variables: 25 #> n: 1,000 #> Basis: pearson #> Tested k: 1-8 #>  #> ── Criteria (k = 1-8) ── #>  #> k = 1: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0246 VSS-1 0.5121 VSS-2 0.0000 CD ✔ #> k = 2: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0190 VSS-1 0.5761 VSS-2 0.6636 CD ✔ #> k = 3: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0172 VSS-1 0.5969 VSS-2 0.7288 CD ✔ #> k = 4: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0164 VSS-1 0.6198* VSS-2 0.7781 CD ✔ #> k = 5: PA-PC ✔ PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0160* VSS-1 0.5730 VSS-2 0.7912* CD ✔ #> k = 6: PA-PC - PA-FA ✔ MAP 0.0170 VSS-1 0.5592 VSS-2 0.7530 CD ✔ #> k = 7: PA-PC - PA-FA - MAP 0.0201 VSS-1 0.5698 VSS-2 0.7290 CD ✔ #> k = 8: PA-PC - PA-FA - MAP 0.0231 VSS-1 0.5615 VSS-2 0.7252 CD ✔* #>  #> ── Recommendations ── #>  #> • PA-PC: k <= 5 #> • PA-FA: k <= 6 #> • MAP: k = 5 #> • VSS-1: k = 4 #> • VSS-2: k = 5 #> • CD: k = 8 #> Consensus range: k = 4-8 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: k_max in ackwards() is a maximum depth. Setting k_max one or two levels #> above the consensus to observe factor fragmentation is intentional. #> Caution: PA-PC tends to overextract; structures may not replicate (Forbes, #> 2023). PA-FA and CD are more conservative. Use the range.

title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: ackwards() fits factor models every level 1 k_max computes -level factor-score correlations define hierarchy.
code: x <- ackwards(bfi25, k_max = 5, cor = "polychoric", missing = "listwise") x #>  #> ── Bass-Ackwards Analysis (ackwards) ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: pca #> Rotation: varimax #> Basis: polychoric #> n: 875 #> k (max): 5 #>  #> ── Levels ── #>  #> ✔ k = 1: 1 factor, 23.2% variance #> ✔ k = 2: 2 factors, 35.5% variance #> ✔ k = 3: 3 factors, 44.6% variance #> ✔ k = 4: 4 factors, 52.2% variance #> ✔ k = 5: 5 factors, 58.4% variance #>  #> ── Edges ── #>  #> 14 of 40 edges have |r| ≥ 0.3 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: This is a series of linked solutions, not a fitted hierarchical model. #> Cross-level edges are descriptive score correlations. Per-level fit indices #> (EFA/ESEM) describe how well a k-factor model fits the items at that level -- #> they do not validate the edges or the hierarchy itself.

title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: serious analysis rarely stops one fit. Three verbs turn hierarchy picture defensible result, one scores new data: comparability(bfi25, k_max = 6) gates hierarchy depth split-half replicability — deepest level every factor re-emerges random halves sample (Everett 1983; Saucier et al. 2005). prune(x, "redundant") flags factors persist across levels without differentiating — Forbes’s (2023) redundancy question. boot_edges(x, bfi25) attaches bootstrap confidence intervals every -level edge. predict(x, newdata) scores observations model never saw, training metric — standard cross-validation pattern. recommended-workflow vignette strings six-step analysis.

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
text: engine = "pca" – original Goldberg (2006) method; fastest; never fails converge; Waller (2007) algebra exact components. rotation = "varimax" – T'=T^-1 property orthogonal rotation enables closed-form W'RW edge algebra keeps within-level factors uncorrelated cross-level edges reflect hierarchical signal. Matches Goldberg (2006), Kim & Eaton (2015), Forbush et al. (2024). Varimax supported rotation; oblique rotation confound -level signal method's core output. cor = "pearson" – silent basis switching. items look ordinal (<= 7 distinct integer values), cli warning suggest cor = "polychoric", available three engines. align_signs = TRUE – unaligned signs make output unreadable. Anchor: m1f1 oriented toward positive manifold; subsequent factor flipped edge primary parent positive. keep_scores = FALSE / keep_fits = FALSE – memory privacy. Scores O(n x Sigmak) often sensitive; raw engine fits can large. recomputable stored r matrix.

text: ackwards() raises diagnostics fits. fall three tiers mean whether trust report solution: Fatal – fix trusting. result undefined rests broken correlation matrix: constant item (variance) errors – drop (see check_items()). psych::polychoric() fails – usually near-empty response category; set correct = 0 collapse rare categories. level fails converge – hierarchy truncated deepest level ; interpret beyond . near-singular correlation matrix (smallest eigenvalue < 1e-4, recorded meta$near_singular / meta$min_eigenvalue re-surfaced print()/summary()) means per-level fit indices factor scores unreliable loadings/edges rest rank-deficient matrix. (EFA residual-based fallback inflates TLI/RMSEA; ESEM CFI comes back NA.) Trim redundant items, use missing = "listwise", – polychoric basis – collapse sparse categories set correct = 0. Caution – interpret carefully. solution exists may unstable: Heywood case (communality > 1 / negative uniqueness) level – check level's loadings make substantive sense; consider fewer factors. near-constant item (one response category dominates) can drive meaningless factor – inspect check_items(). Ordinal data Pearson basis attenuates correlations – fine quick look suggest_k() screening, report final model cor = "polychoric". Informational – usually fine. Proceed, just aware: pairwise-missing note, merely sparse (rare--present) response category, ordinal-detection warning intend Pearson.

title: Plot a bass-ackwards diagram or per-level fit index chart — autoplot.ackwards
code: # \donttest{ if (requireNamespace("ggplot2", quietly = TRUE)) {   # sim16 has a known 1 -> 2 -> 4 hierarchy (continuous; pearson basis)   x <- ackwards(sim16, k_max = 5)   autoplot(x)   autoplot(x, color_pos = "steelblue")    # Encode sign by linetype instead of colour, or by both   autoplot(x, sign_by = "linetype")   autoplot(x, sign_by = "both")    # Left-to-right layout (wide slides / posters)   autoplot(x, direction = "horizontal")    # Per-level fit index chart (EFA or ESEM only)   x_efa <- ackwards(sim16, k_max = 5, engine = "efa")   autoplot(x_efa, what = "fit")    # Monochrome with correlation labels (for greyscale figures)   autoplot(x, mono = TRUE, show_r = TRUE)    # Custom node labels for the 5-factor level   autoplot(x, node_labels = c(m5f1 = "Factor A", m5f2 = "Factor B"))    # Primary links only -- clean hierarchy tree   autoplot(x, primary_only = TRUE)    # Forbes pruned view: omit redundant nodes, straight spanning arrows   xp <- ackwards(sim16, k_max = 5) |> prune("redundant")   autoplot(xp, drop_pruned = TRUE)   autoplot(xp, drop_pruned = TRUE, show_r = TRUE)   autoplot(xp, drop_pruned = TRUE, compress_levels = TRUE)    # Plain line ends without arrowheads   autoplot(x, show_arrows = FALSE)    # Uniform edge width (no |r| scaling)   autoplot(x, edge_linewidth = 0.5)    # Suppress legend   autoplot(x, legend = FALSE)    # Forbes (2023) publication style: black lines, uniform width, no arrowheads   autoplot(xp,     drop_pruned = TRUE,     color_pos = "black", color_neg = "black",     edge_linewidth = 0.6, show_arrows = FALSE, legend = FALSE   ) } #> ℹ Redundancy pruning (direct criterion, |r| ≥ 0.9) flagged 7 nodes. #> ℹ Nodes are retained in the object; inspect with `x$prune$nodes` and #>   `x$prune$chains`.  # }

what: Screen a dataset for factorability and sampling adequacy — factorability
title: Screen a dataset for factorability and sampling adequacy — factorability
text: extracting hierarchy, worth asking two prior questions: correlation matrix even worth factoring, sample large enough trust answer. factorability() reports standard diagnostics , one object can print pull values : KMO – Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin measure sampling adequacy, overall per variable (via psych::KMO()). contrasts correlations partial correlations: low value means variables share little common variance variables partialled , factor model little recover. Bartlett's test sphericity – tests whether correlation matrix differs identity (via psych::cortest.bartlett()). Needs N; non-significant result says correlations weak factor. Sample size – N, number variables p, N:p ratio. Ledermann bound – largest number common factors identifiable p variables (hard limit EFA/ESEM, though PCA). Read conventions, verdicts. Every cutoff – Kaiser's KMO bands, 5:1 / 10:1 N:p rules thumb, Bartlett significance .05 – widely repeated rule thumb, settled threshold, contested methodological literature (required N particular depends communalities factor overdetermination far fixed ratio; MacCallum et al. 1999). factorability() deliberately reports numbers conventional bands rather returning pass/fail flag. ackwards() runs screen internally warns genuinely consequential extreme (KMO < 0.5, N:p < 5).

title: Flag redundant or artifactual factors (Forbes 2023 extension) — prune
text: x ackwards object. ... Reserved future methods/arguments. rules Character vector controlling auto-rules run. Default "none" (auto rule; combine manual pure manual pruning, call prune(x) arguments clear existing pruning). Options: "redundant" – identify chains factors connected score correlations |r| >= redundancy_r (optionally phi > redundancy_phi), using redundancy_criterion (default "direct", faithful Forbes). Applies Forbes's (2023) retention rule: keep bottom node chain reaches level k_max (specific); keep top node otherwise. Flagged nodes get pruned = TRUE prune_reason = "redundant" x$prune$nodes. "artifact" (alias "artefact", normalized "artifact") – compute Tucker's congruence coefficient (phi) cross-level factor pairs store x$prune$phi, plus structural signals (few_items/orphan/split_merge) x$prune$structural. factors auto-flagged; artifact identification requires judgment (Forbes, 2023; Wicherts et al., 2016). manual Character vector factor labels (e.g. c("m4f3", "m4f4")) flag directly, addition instead auto rule. Standalone manual pruning supported: prune(x, manual = c("m4f3")). Unknown labels error. node already flagged auto rule keeps prune_reason; otherwise-unflagged manual nodes get prune_reason = "manual". redundancy_r Scalar (0, 1]. Score-correlation |r| threshold redundancy chains. Default 0.9 (Forbes, 2023). redundancy_phi Scalar (0, 1], NULL (default, auto), NA (explicit opt-). NULL: x$engine == "pca" – phi filter. Component scores determinate (exact linear functions data, factor-score indeterminacy), score correlation |r| true correlation components ; phi adds nothing |r| already capture. x$engine "efa" "esem" – automatically set 0.95 (Lorenzo-Seva & ten Berge, 2006). Factor-score indeterminacy -PCA means |r|-alone liberal; conjunctive phi criterion conservative default. cli message announces resolved value. Pass NA disable phi filtering regardless engine. Pass numeric value override engine. redundancy_criterion redundancy chains traced. One : "direct" (default) – chase upward via direct (skip-level) correlation factor ancestor level, continuing |r| >= redundancy_r contiguously. title: Simulated continuous bass-ackwards teaching example (16 items, known hierarchy) — sim16
text: Population model. Sigma = Lambda %*% Phi %*% t(Lambda) + Psi, oblique common-factor model 4 true group factors, sampled via Cholesky factorization (base R; MASS dependency): items load 0.75 true factor (cross-loadings). Factor correlations: 0.45 within metatrait (f1-f2, f3-f4), 0.15 metatraits. Uniquenesses 1 - communality (uniform 0.4375 symmetry design ). Ground-truth hierarchy (verified ackwards(engine = "efa")): k=1 recovers single general factor across 16 items; k=2 splits along metatrait line (i1-i8 vs. i9-i16); k=4 recovers 4 true group factors exactly; six suggest_k() recommendations (five criteria – VSS reports complexities 1 2) reach consensus k = 4. Idealized design. planted signal strong clean, six suggest_k() recommendations converge k = 4 – deliberately easy case, building intuition recovering known hierarchy looks like. Real data rarely agree cleanly: bfi25 criteria span k = 4–6. two datasets complementary teaching foils – sim16 "watch method recover structure planted," bfi25 "reason hierarchy criteria disagree." Present sim16's consensus ideal, norm. Deliberate overextraction artifact k=5. population exactly 4 factors, requesting 5th finds real dimension: EFA produces orphan factor zero primary-loading items. prune(x, "artifact") (default min_items = 3, orphan_r = 0.5), factor flagged few_items orphan. true (non- splitting) factors persist essentially unchanged k=3 onward, parent-child score correlations approach 1 flagged prune(x, "redundant") (|r| >= .9 , EFA auto-default, Tucker's phi > .95). textbook overextraction artifact, included Forbes/redundancy examples guaranteed finding teach (unlike bfi25, reliably trigger one). regenerate dataset, run source("data-raw/sim16.R") package root (set.seed(42)).

text: data data frame numeric matrix (items columns, observations rows). Alternatively, pre-computed correlation matrix may supplied (square, symmetric, numeric matrix unit diagonal). correlation matrix supplied, n_obs required (PA VSS need N), cor argument ignored, Comparison Data (CD) criterion skipped (CD requires raw item distributions resampling). k_max Maximum number factors/components evaluate recommending depth – depth . Defaults min(ncol(data) - 1, 8). Increase expect deeper hierarchy. (Note: distinct meaning ackwards()'s k_max, extraction depth; two share name dial suggest_k() -> ackwards() workflow, just applied different stages.) criteria Character vector criteria compute. subset c("pa_pc", "pa_fa", "map", "vss", "cd"); default five. "pa_pc" "pa_fa" share one parallel-analysis call (neither fast); "map" "vss" share one VSS call. Criteria requested skipped entirely (computation) k_* fields result NA. "vss" selects VSS-1 VSS-2 unit. cor Correlation basis: "pearson" (default) "spearman". match cor argument plan use ackwards(). Ignored data correlation matrix (basis already fixed). n_obs Number observations. Required data pre-computed correlation matrix (PA VSS need N). Ignored raw data supplied (N determined nrow(data)). n_iter Number Monte Carlo iterations parallel analysis. Default 20. Reduce 5 fast/exploratory runs; increase 100+ publication. seed Integer seed passed set.seed() Comparison Data (CD) step. NULL (default) uses current RNG state. Note: parallel-analysis step uses psych::fa.parallel(), respond reliably set.seed() – PA simulation results vary across calls regardless seed. ... Reserved future arguments.

title: Suggest a maximum number of factors for bass-ackwards analysis — suggest_k
text: object class "suggest_k". Print formatted summary; call autoplot() diagnostic scree/criteria plot. list contains: k_parallel_pc Recommended k PC-based parallel analysis (NA_integer_ "pa_pc" criteria). k_parallel_fa Recommended k FA-based parallel analysis (NA_integer_ FA factor exceeded random threshold, "pa_fa" criteria). k_map Recommended k MAP (NA_integer_ "map" criteria). k_vss1 Recommended k VSS complexity-1 (NA_integer_ "vss" criteria). k_vss2 Recommended k VSS complexity-2 (NA_integer_ "vss" criteria). k_cd Recommended k Comparison Data (NA_integer_ "cd" criteria, EFAtools installed, CD fails). cd_available Logical; TRUE "cd" requested, EFAtools found, CD ran successfully. criteria Data frame one row per k: k, ev_obs (observed PC eigenvalue), ev_obs_fa (observed FA eigenvalue), pa_pc_quant / pa_fa_quant (95th-pct simulated eigenvalue basis), pa_pc_suggested / pa_fa_suggested (logical retention), map, vss1, vss2. Columns non-requested criteria NA. criteria_requested Character vector criteria requested (therefore computed). k_max, n_obs, n_vars, cor Metadata.

title: Suggest a maximum number of factors for bass-ackwards analysis — suggest_k
text: Criteria available (controlled criteria argument): PA-PC ("pa_pc") – Horn (1965) parallel analysis PC eigenvalues. Compares observed eigenvalues random correlation matrices; suggests retaining components whose eigenvalues exceed 95th percentile chance. Tends overextract; treat upper bound. PA-FA ("pa_fa") – Horn (1965) parallel analysis using common-factor eigenvalues. conservative PA-PC better match EFA ESEM engines ackwards(). Shares one psych::fa.parallel() call PA-PC. MAP ("map") – Velicer (1976) Minimum Average Partial criterion. Finds k minimises average squared partial correlation remaining extracting k components. Usually conservative. Shares one psych::vss() call VSS. VSS ("vss") – Revelle & Rocklin (1979) Simple Structure fit complexities 1 2 (VSS-1 VSS-2). Finds k maximising fit simple loading structure. Shares one psych::vss() call MAP. CD ("cd") – Ruscio & Roche (2012) Comparison Data. Resamples observed item distributions generate comparison eigenvalue profiles; retains factors adding one longer improves RMSE beyond chance. Requires EFAtools package (install separately). Skipped informational message EFAtools absent correlation matrix supplied. PA (bases), MAP, VSS share correlation matrix ackwards(). CD operates raw data matrix directly (required resampling).

title: Suggest a maximum number of factors for bass-ackwards analysis — suggest_k
text: k ackwards() maximum depth, claim exactly k factors exist. Users commonly set k one two levels consensus watch higher-level factors fragment – feature method, overextraction. Treating retention estimates range rather verdict direct support parallel-analysis literature: Lim Jahng (2019) recommend interpreting PA estimate range roughly plus minus one factor, resolved interpretability, Achim (2021) argues even overstates PA's precision – disagreement suggest_k() reports several criteria consensus range, never single number.

title: Suggest a maximum number of factors for bass-ackwards analysis — suggest_k
text: suggest_k() screens Pearson Spearman basis design never computes polychoric correlations , raw data look ordinal (7 distinct integer values per column) emits one-per-session warning – detect_ordinal() signal ackwards() comparability() use (Invariant 6: announce consequential defaults loudly). Crucially, advice points final ackwards() fit (cor = "polychoric"), suggest_k() : screening basis intentional. plausible-k range robust Pearson-vs-polychoric choice, screening Pearson fitting final model polychoric recommended workflow – computing polychoric correlations screening stage add cost NPD risk without changing range. warning skipped correlation-matrix input (items inspect).

PA-FA CD conservative. Treat full set criteria range: true k likely somewhere middle.

Per-level fit indices #> (EFA/ESEM) describe how well a k-factor model fits the items at that level -- #> they do not validate the edges or the hierarchy itself.

code: # S3 method for class 'ackwards' tidy(   x,   what = c("edges", "loadings", "variance", "fit", "nodes", "scores"),   primary_only = FALSE,   sort = c("none", "strength"),   format = c("long", "wide"),   conf_level = 0.95,   ... )

title: Tidy an ackwards object into a long data frame — tidy.ackwards
text: x ackwards object. extract: "edges" (default) – one row per directed -level edge: , , level_from, level_to, r, is_primary, above_cut. boot_edges() run object, four bootstrap columns appended: se, lo, hi (bootstrap standard error percentile confidence-interval endpoints), n_boot_ok (usable replicates). "loadings" – one row per item x factor x level: level, factor, item, loading, se, ci_lower, ci_upper. se, ci_lower, ci_upper populated engine = "esem" (produces rotation-aware loading SEs); NA PCA EFA. confidence level controlled conf_level. "variance" – one row per factor x level: level, factor, proportion, cumulative. proportions total item variance 0-1 scale (multiply 100 percentage). "fit" – one row per fit statistic x level: level, statistic, value. PCA objects statistics eigenvalues; EFA objects chi, dof, p_value, RMSEA, TLI, BIC – chi likelihood-ratio chi-square (psych::fa()'s STATISTIC), chi, dof, p_value, RMSEA, TLI share one statistical framing (psych's residual-based empirical chi-square different statistic reported); ESEM chi, dof, p_value, CFI, TLI, RMSEA, SRMR, BIC. ESEM scaled-test estimator ("WLSMV"/"ULSMV" ordinal data, "MLR" continuous), whole row – chi/dof/ p_value CFI/TLI/RMSEA – reports lavaan's mean--variance-adjusted ("scaled") variant, every quantity shares one scaling. matters WLSMV/ULSMV: naive chi-square valid reference distribution (lavaan's summary() labels p-value "Unknown"), naive CFI/TLI badly optimistic ordinal data (Xia & Yang, 2019). "ML" scaled variant, reports naive values (correct ones ML). SRMR scaled variant reported -. BIC NA WLSMV/ULSMV (proper log-likelihood limited-information estimator) populated ML/MLR. Use format = "wide" one row per non-anchor level (k >= 2; saturated 1-factor anchor dropped, matching summary() autoplot(= "fit")), one column per statistic. Conventional fit cutoffs (Hu & Bentler 1999) shown reference lines autoplot(= "fit") inline summary(), returned pass/fail column – contested thresholds, report-, never gate anything (see functions' docs). format oriented EFA/ESEM model-fit statistics; PCA "statistics" per-component eigenvalues. "nodes" – Forbes-extension pruning annotations (requires prune != "none" object created). One row per factor across levels: id, level, pruned, prune_reason. Returns empty data frame columns pruning applied. "scores" – long-format per-observation factor scores (requires keep_scores = TRUE fit time use augment.ackwards() --fly computation). Columns: obs (row index), level, factor, score. primary_only = "edges" . TRUE, returns just factor's primary-parent edge (is_primary == TRUE) – lineage tree diagram draws solid arrows. Default FALSE (edges). Errors value . sort = "edges" . One "none" (default, natural order) "strength" (descending |r|). Ignored values . format = "fit" . One "long" (default, one row per statistic x level) "wide" (one row per level, one column per statistic). Errors values . conf_level = "loadings" . Confidence level loading intervals; default 0.95. intervals computed loading ± qnorm((1 + conf_level) / 2) * se NA engines carry SEs (PCA, EFA). Errors values . ... Ignored.

title: Display the salient items for each factor — top_items
text: x ackwards object. level Integer vector selecting level(s) include. NULL (default) returns levels. cut Absolute-loading threshold. Items |loading| >= cut shown. Default 0.3, matching cut_show plotting default. n Maximum number items show per factor. NULL (default) shows items meeting cut. set, top-n items |loading| kept applying cut. sort Logical. TRUE (default), items within group ordered descending |loading|. Set FALSE keep original order (useful items meaningful sequence). One "factor" (default) "item". "factor" groups listing factor (salient items factor – "factor ?"). "item" inverts grouping list, item, factors loads – makes cross-loadings legible ("item go?"). n sort apply within whichever unit selects. show_labels Logical. TRUE (default) data carried variable-label attribute fit time (see ackwards()), items shown id: label; items without label fall back bare id. Set FALSE always show bare m{k}f{j}-style item ids.

title: ackwards 0.1.1
text: CRAN resubmission first release, addressing reviewer feedback 0.1.0 submission; also picks everything added since submission. Fixed compatibility lavaan 0.7. lavaan 0.7 renamed sample-statistics slot argument (breaking ESEM engine’s multi-level reuse anchor-level sample statistics — every level beyond k = 1 failed build) now requires explicit ordered = FALSE use WLSMV/ULSMV continuous data. ESEM engine detects installed lavaan’s argument vocabulary works lavaan >= 0.7 >= 0.6-13. New vignette: “Reproducing Forbes (2023): AMH Applied Example” (vignette("ackwards-forbes2023")). full worked reproduction paper’s 155-variable applied example bundled forbes2023 dataset: 10-level hierarchy, skip-level correlations, redundancy chase (including default direct criterion adjacent opt-disagree), pruned-factor diagram paper’s publication style. Corrected Forbes (2023) article title forbes2023 help page (“bass-ackward method”, per published title). Corrected n_obs advisory PCA correlation-matrix input. message (n_obs help text) claimed supplying n_obs enable chi-square/RMSEA/TLI, PCA engine’s level fit eigenvalue-based never computes ; n_obs recorded result metadata feeds N-based sampling-adequacy checks . message now says . behavior changes. Completed Goldberg (2006) reference full published title (“Bass-Ackwards: development hierarchical factor structures top ”) ackwards() help page four vignettes. Corrected historical citations comparability()’s split-half benchmarks. .90 replication threshold traces Everett (1983) use Goldberg’s lexical research program Saucier (1997) Saucier, Georgiades, Tsaousis, Goldberg (2005) — Goldberg (1990), contains split-half analyses longer cited purpose. .95 reference line now sourced Lorenzo-Seva ten Berge (2006). Affects roxygen help page, print()/autoplot() footer text, README, replicability-workflow vignette; behavior changes. Sourced suggest_k()’s k-selection guidance. consensus-range stance now cites Lim Jahng (2019) Achim’s (2021) counterpoint, “PA-PC tends overextract” note cites Saucier (1997) alongside Forbes (2023), help page suggest-k vignette; behavior changes. label_template() now returns scaffold visibly (behavior change). named character vector carries class "ackwards_labels", editable c(...) literal rendered print() method instead written console unconditionally. top-level call looks ; assigning result (labs <- label_template(x)) passing inline (e.g. inside autoplot()) now silent, per CRAN policy console output. DESCRIPTION now spells principal component analysis (PCA), exploratory factor analysis (EFA), exploratory structural equation modeling (ESEM) per CRAN feedback. New bundled dataset forbes2023. 155-variable “Assessing Mental Health” Spearman correlation matrix forms Forbes’s (2023) applied example now exported, ackwards(forbes2023, k_max = 10) reproduces worked hierarchy directly. joins bfi25 sim16. matrix redistributed CC-4.0 attribution M. K. Forbes (see LICENSE.note). prune("redundant") gains redundancy_criterion, defaulting "direct" (behavior change). Redundancy chains now traced direct (skip-level) correlation factor ancestor level — Forbes’s actual ChaseCorrPaths rule — rather previous adjacent-hop walk. correlation non-transitive, two can differ deep (many-level) hierarchies: shallow ones (e.g. bundled sim16) results unchanged, factor can now flagged redundant ancestor correlates directly even intermediate step weak (vice versa). Pass redundancy_criterion = "adjacent" old behavior. makes prune("redundant") reproduce Forbes’s published applied example exactly. print() summary() name active criterion. Note "direct", chain’s r_to_prev column reports adjacent-level correlation can sit redundancy_r (membership set direct skip-level link; see ?prune). Validation. Forbes (2023) fidelity suite now also reproduces 155-variable “Assessing Mental Health” applied example (k_max = 10), just three simulation studies: -level correlations match reference implementation 1.3e-14 across 45 level-pairs, loading congruences agree within two-decimal rounding, redundancy chase reproduced 54 components. published matrix ships test fixture CC-4.0 (see LICENSE.note). suggest_k() now reads Comparison Data (CD) results EFAtools >= 0.8.0, restructured CD()’s return value (per-iteration RMSE matrix moved top-level RMSE_eigenvalues field results[[1]]$rmse_eigenvalues). Without , CD criterion autoplot() panel silently dropped current EFAtools installed. Older EFAtools versions still work. Console output consistency. summary()’s per-level fit-index pass/fail mark now terminal-adaptive cli glyph print()’s convergence mark (tick/cross degrades v/x non-UTF-8 console), replacing hard-coded Unicode ✔/✘. print()’s cumulative-variance percentages now carry fixed single decimal (e.g. 20.0%, whole-number value previously printed 20%), matching summary(). Cosmetic ; reported values unchanged.

title: ackwards 0.1.0
text: First public release. ackwards implements Goldberg’s (2006) bass-ackwards method modern extensions: fits factor solutions every level 1 k characterizes hierarchy -level factor-score correlations connect . Initial features, roughly order importance: ackwards() — fit hierarchy PCA, EFA, ESEM engine; -level edges Waller’s (2007) exact W′RW algebra. suggest_k() — bracket plausible depth range five retention criteria (parallel analysis, MAP, VSS, Comparison Data). comparability() — gate hierarchy depth split-half replicability (Everett 1983; Goldberg 1990). factorability() — screen dataset (correlation matrix) fit: Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin sampling adequacy (overall per item), Bartlett’s test sphericity, N:p ratio, Ledermann bound identifiable factors, reported numbers--bands rather pass/fail. ackwards() runs light version internally warns consequential extreme (k_max Ledermann bound EFA/ESEM, poor sampling adequacy). Forbes (2023) extension — pairs = "" skip-level edges, prune() redundant/artifactual factors, boot_edges() bootstrap edge CIs. Ordinal data — cor = "polychoric" (WLSMV ESEM), check_items() pre-analysis screening, near-singularity diagnostics. Interpretation & scoring — top_items() reading factors; label_template()/set_factor_labels()/factor_labels() attach persistent substantive names shown across print(), summary(), autoplot(), tidy(), top_items(); augment()/predict() factor scores, sample. Output — autoplot() hierarchy diagrams tidy()/glance()/summary(). Bundled data — bfi25 (ordinal Big Five) sim16 (continuous), plus eight vignettes. Beyond base R, psych hard dependency; lavaan, ggplot2, others optional.
